subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Expanded National Government Powers and Policy Making
stimulus:
The United States Constitution establishes a federal system of government. Under federalism, policy making is shared between national and state governments. Over time, the powers of the national government have increased relative to those of the state governments.

Develop an argument about whether the expanded powers of the national government benefits or hinders policy making.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: The Articles of Confederation, Brutus 1, The Federalist 10.)
parts.scoring_notes: Restating the prompt does not earn the point. The thesis must take a clear position and establish a line of reasoning. It may be located anywhere in the response.

scoring_points.description: Row A — Thesis/Claim: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “The expanded powers of the national government have made it more efficient, and if states had more power it would be dangerous.”

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “The expanded powers of the national government benefit policy making because of the strength of the Constitution, the increase of cooperative federalism, and the advantages of fiscal federalism.”

wrong_examples: “Because of my knowledge of the United States Government, I believe the expanded powers of the national government benefit policy making.” Does not respond to the prompt:
“The federal government has expanded power over time.” Responses that earn this point:
The claim or thesis responds to the prompt rather than restating or rephrasing the prompt and establishes a line of reasoning.
The response includes a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning about whether the expanded powers of national government benefits or hinders policy making.
common_traps: Restating the prompt without making a claim
Describing the issue without taking a position

parts.prompt: Support your claim or thesis with at least TWO pieces of accurate and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
parts.scoring_notes: To earn 2 or 3 points, the response must have a defensible claim/thesis (Row A). To earn 3 points, one piece of evidence must be from the listed foundational documents (Articles of Confederation, Brutus 1, or Federalist 10).

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (0 points): No accurate evidence or evidence not relevant to the topic.

alternatives.correct_example: No points earned — evidence is inaccurate or irrelevant to the prompt.

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (1 point): Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt.

required_elements: One piece of evidence relevant to the topic.
alternatives.correct_example: One piece of evidence relevant to the topic.

common_traps: Evidence must be relevant but need not directly support a specific claim to earn 1 point

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (2 points): Uses one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim/thesis OR provides two pieces of relevant evidence.

required_elements: One specific and relevant piece of evidence supports the claim.
alternatives.correct_example: One specific and relevant piece of evidence supports the claim.

common_traps: To earn 2 points, the response must have earned Row A (claim/thesis)

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (3 points): Uses two pieces of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim, one from a listed foundational document.

required_elements: Two pieces of specific relevant evidence, one from a foundational document listed in the prompt.
alternatives.correct_example: Two pieces of specific relevant evidence, one from a foundational document listed in the prompt.

common_traps: One piece must be from a foundational document listed in the prompt

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
parts.scoring_notes: To earn this point, the response must have a defensible claim/thesis (Row A) and at least one piece of specific and relevant evidence (at least 2 points in Row B). Simply listing evidence without explaining the connection to the thesis does not earn this point.

scoring_points.description: Row C — Reasoning: Use reasoning (classification, process, causation, or comparison) to explain how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: Include evidence but offer no reasoning to connect the evidence to the claim or thesis

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: Restate the prompt without explaining how the evidence supports the claim or thesis Response that earns this point:

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: Must explain the relationship between the evidence provided and the claim or thesis Examples that earn this point:

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “The Articles of Confederation made it harder to pass laws because each state has very different agendas, and it required over a majority of them to agree. To amend, it required a unanimous decision. This makes it nearly impossible to add a new amendment to change policy.”

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “This efficiency and accountability from a strong executive benefits policy making.” Additional Notes:

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: To earn this point, the response must have a defensible claim or thesis (earned the point in Row A) and support that argument with at least one piece of specific and relevant evidence (earned at least 2 points in Row B).

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: The explanation of the relationship between one piece of evidence and the claim or thesis is sufficient to earn this point.

common_traps: Must have earned Row A and at least 2 points in Row B
Simply restating evidence without reasoning does not earn this point

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternative perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
parts.scoring_notes: To earn this point, the response must have a defensible claim/thesis (Row A). Restating the opposite of the thesis does not earn the point. Identifying an alternate perspective without refuting, conceding, or rebutting it does not earn the point. Refuting a foundational document rather than an alternate perspective does not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Row D — Responds to Alternate Perspectives: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: Must describe an alternate perspective AND rebut or refute that perspective.

common_traps: Simply identifying an alternate perspective without rebutting/refuting it does not earn this point
